about.bio1: I'm a senior digital product designer with over 8 years of experience crafting digital experiences that matter. My approach combines strategic thinking with pixel-perfect execution, ensuring every design decision serves both user needs and business goals.
about.bio2: I believe great design is invisible—it simply works. My process is rooted in empathy, validated by research, and refined through iteration. I've had the privilege of working with startups and Fortune 500 companies alike, helping them transform complex challenges into intuitive solutions.
about.philosophyQuote: “Design is not just what it looks like and feels like. Design is how it works.” — Steve Jobs

contact.subtitle: Have a project in mind? I'd love to hear about it. Drop me a message and let's start the conversation.

hero.title: Arcatext
hero.subtitle: A translation keyboard that turns real conversations into the lesson — then gives you the insight to grow.

intro: Arcatext is a language-learning product designed to make the complexity and nuance of language more accessible — and the learning process more natural, engaging, and practical.

The first phase, a translation keyboard, helps users turn real conversations with friends, penpals, and AI into personalized language-learning experiences. Instead of forcing learners through generic lessons, Arcatext helps them learn from the conversations they actually want to have.
It combines context-aware translation, clarification tools, and language exposure so users can communicate across languages with greater fluency, accuracy, and confidence.
origin: Arcatext began as a personal attempt to make multilingual texting feel more fluid. Early exploration exposed the friction of existing translator keyboards and copy-paste workflows, which shifted the project from a conceptual messaging app optimization into a true product able to create value in any messaging app.
overview.readingWritingTitle: The focus on reading and writing
readingWriting: Language learning involves reading, writing, speaking, listening, vocabulary, grammar, and tone. While many tools support guided lessons or speaking practice, the biggest opportunity was in reading and writing — especially in real texting conversations.

Messaging gives learners time to pause, revise, compare, and understand language before responding. That made it the ideal entry point: a way to turn everyday conversations into personalized learning moments.
role: I led Arcatext as a founder-designer, taking the product from early concept through research, MVP design, implementation, pricing strategy, and launch preparation.

My role spanned product strategy, UX architecture, interaction design, AI behavior design, front-end and back-end development, and business formation. I created the LLC, developed the iOS app, designed the core translation keyboard experience, and built the foundation for Arcatext to evolve from an MVP into a broader language-learning platform.

A major shift in the project was learning to work across strategy, design, technology, and business at the same time. Product direction, technical feasibility, AI behavior, implementation, pricing, and interaction design were not separate tracks — they constantly shaped each other.

It also deepened my understanding of how design and development depend on each other. There were many features and interaction details I initially wanted to include, but building and testing them in code exposed reliability risks, edge cases, or implementation costs that changed the design direction.

problem.titleLead: Most language-learning tools separate practice from
intro: Learners spend time on scripted exercises, flashcards, grammar drills, and guided lessons — but those activities don’t always prepare them for the conversations they actually want to have. Real communication is personal, unpredictable, emotional, and full of nuance.

A learner may know vocabulary from an app but still struggle when texting a friend, replying to a penpal, navigating tone, choosing the right level of formality, reading an unfamiliar script, or understanding why one translation sounds natural and another does not.

Translation tools solve part of the problem, but they usually focus on producing a result rather than helping the user learn from the moment. They translate the message, but rarely expose the decisions behind the translation.
problem.opportunityLead: What if everyday messages could become personalized

problems.title: Learning is disconnected from real life
problems.desc: Scripted exercises rarely prepare learners for the unpredictable, personal conversations they actually want to have.

problems.title: Generic paths over personal goals
problems.desc: Guided systems decide what to learn next, even when those lessons ignore the relationships and topics that motivate the user.

problems.title: Translators solve the message, not the moment
problems.desc: Most tools produce a result without explaining why a translation works — or how meaning shifts through tone, gender, or context.

problems.title: Nuance is hidden in a black box
problems.desc: Users often need to adjust gender, register, or word choice, but those decisions are buried inside the translation engine.

problems.title: Unfamiliar scripts add a second barrier
problems.desc: For Japanese, Arabic, Hindi, Korean, Thai, Armenian, and others, the writing system is its own challenge on top of the language.

opportunities.title: Use natural conversations as lessons
opportunities.desc: Every message becomes a chance to learn vocabulary, grammar, tone, and cultural nuance in context.

opportunities.title: Give users control over meaning
opportunities.desc: Treat translation as a dialogue — clarify ambiguity, compare alternatives, adjust nuance.

opportunities.title: Turn translation into feedback
opportunities.desc: Reverse translation, synonyms, and homograph clarification build confidence instead of blind copying.

opportunities.title: Bridge scripts with romanization
opportunities.desc: Romanization connects meaning, pronunciation, and native script — letting learners participate before full literacy.

opportunities.title: Learn from real behavior
opportunities.desc: Real conversations reveal recurring words, grammar gaps, and topics — the foundation for an adaptive learning path.

closing: Before Arcatext, practicing through texting often meant a multi-step process just to get a translation: translating outgoing messages, using separate tools to understand incoming messages, re-translating earlier messages to maintain context, and still having limited control over gender, homographs, script, and tone.
Arcatext reduces that friction by bringing translation, context, and clarification into one integrated writing experience.

strategy.titleLead: Balancing the learner, the product, the system, and the
intro: Product strategy meant constantly weighing the relationship between the learner, the product experience, the technical system, and the market — prioritizing what would reduce friction now while setting up the product for a broader language-learning platform later.
second: Starting with a keyboard gave Arcatext the most versatile entry point into the learner’s communication life. Instead of asking users to practice in one new destination, the keyboard could plug into daily texting with friends and penpals, language-exchange apps, dating apps, email, and AI chatbots. This allowed me to test the core learning hypothesis — that real texting can become effective language practice — across many existing contexts before investing in a dedicated AI conversation platform for more focused learning.
strategy.quoteLead: “The keyboard is not just a translation shortcut. It is the
strategy.quoteHighlight: entry point into a larger learning system.”

cards.title: Start where learners already communicate
cards.desc: Build Arcatext as a keyboard so it works inside iMessage, WhatsApp, HelloTalk, Instagram, dating apps, and email — available at the exact moment a user needs help.

cards.title: Messaging app vs. keyboard
cards.desc: Evaluated building a new messaging app, but learners were unlikely to move existing conversations or convince contacts to switch platforms. The keyboard meets users where their relationships already exist.

cards.title: Competitive review
cards.desc: Existing translation keyboards introduced cost, usability, or reliability issues. That pushed the product toward a purpose-built keyboard focused on one-tap translation and minimal interruption to the texting flow.

cards.title: Serve multiple language levels
cards.desc: Beginners get a safe communication assistant. Intermediates get a feedback tool. Advanced learners get a nuance tool. The product grows with the user.

cards.title: Leverage real relationships first
cards.desc: Begin with the conversations users already care about — friends, penpals, classmates, partners, exchange partners — then expand into 24/7 AI conversations once the core learning loop is established.

cards.title: Build toward a personalized learning system
cards.desc: Repeated communication becomes structured insight: vocabulary, grammar patterns, translation choices, confidence gaps. The foundation for adaptive practice and teacher tools.

artifact: To map the product beyond the MVP, I created a strategy canvas organizing Arcatext across four phases: an integrated translation keyboard, an AI conversation platform, data-driven learning insights, and teacher tools. The canvas mapped target users, competitive disruption, feature evolution, revenue streams, and long-term AI infrastructure — connecting near-term UX decisions to a scalable product strategy.

loop.desc: Start with something the user actually wants to say — to a friend, a penpal, a class, or an AI.
Translate or refine the message while preserving intent, tone, and language-specific nuance.
Inspect the result before sending. Reverse translation and alternates build trust in the output.
Resolve ambiguity — homographs, gender, formality, script — so meaning stays in the user’s control.
Turn each interaction into vocabulary, grammar, and confidence the user can build on over time.

closing: The product system is designed to make translation useful in the moment while also turning that moment into learning. The Reword experience supports context-aware translation, grammar correction, tone refinement, and language-specific options like speaker gender, recipient gender, group gender, and script preferences. The Check experience helps users verify meaning before sending, so they avoid blindly sending text they don’t understand.

intro: Every interaction needed to preserve the flow of texting rather than pull the user into a separate learning task.

items.title: Embed learning inside the writing surface
items.desc: A keyboard sits inside iMessage, WhatsApp, HelloTalk, Instagram, dating apps, email — meeting users where their relationships already exist instead of asking them to leave the conversation.

items.title: Treat translation as a learning interaction
items.desc: Most translators present a single output and expect trust. Arcatext lets users inspect, compare, reverse-check, and clarify — shifting the product from translator to learning companion.

items.title: Make nuance controllable
items.desc: Expose hidden translation decisions: gender, tone, formality, script, regional variation. Translation isn’t one fixed answer — it’s a set of meaning decisions.

items.title: Support confidence before sending
items.desc: The Check experience reduces the fear of sending something wrong, awkward, or unintended — supporting both communication and learning confidence.

items.title: Design for multiple fluency levels
items.desc: Beginner: a safe communication assistant. Intermediate: a feedback tool. Advanced: a nuance tool. The product reveals depth as the user grows.

items.title: Use real conversations as the curriculum
items.desc: Instead of a fixed sequence, the user’s actual writing becomes the source of learning — making the path personal, relevant, and emotionally meaningful.

execution.discoveryTitle: Conducted discovery interviews with learners and teachers
discovery: I spoke with language students, language teachers, and language-exchange app users to understand how they were currently learning, practicing, and teaching languages. I explored the tools they relied on, where existing methods broke down, what made conversation practice difficult, and what support they needed in real communication contexts.

These conversations helped validate the opportunity around texting-based practice and shaped early product decisions — including the shift away from a standalone messaging app and toward a keyboard that could support learners inside the apps they already used.
synthesis: To keep early product direction grounded in user needs, I translated interview notes and quotes into affinity themes — tracing each insight back to real user pain points instead of relying on assumptions about what language learners might want.

The strongest pattern was that learners did not need another generic practice environment. They needed a way to close the gap between learning and real conversation. Practice had to feel relevant to their goals, reflect the vocabulary and grammar they actually wanted to use, and support either real conversations or highly customizable conversational practice.

Further concept exploration, user feedback, market research, and language study helped narrow the strategy. There was overlap across users, but some needs were language-specific — additional support for non-Roman scripts, gendered grammar, and translation context. Studying translation tools also revealed a broader product gap: the complexity and nuance of language was not well supported, and users often had to accept generic outputs because tools lacked meaningful controls.

directionalInsights.title: Current apps did not fully prepare learners for real-world conversation
directionalInsights.desc: Language apps were useful for habit-building, vocabulary, and structured practice, but scripted content often failed to prepare learners for unpredictable, personal, context-dependent conversations.

directionalInsights.title: Generic content did not always align with learner goals
directionalInsights.desc: Learners had specific reasons for studying — travel, relationships, work, school, cultural connection, or communication with a specific person. When lesson content did not match those goals, practice felt less relevant and less motivating.

directionalInsights.title: Real conversations were motivating, but poorly supported
directionalInsights.desc: Students and language-exchange users were often most engaged when practice connected to real people and real topics. But the tools around those conversations were fragmented.

directionalInsights.title: Switching between tools created too much friction
directionalInsights.desc: Learners often had to move between messaging apps, translators, keyboards, and reverse-translation tools just to understand and respond to a message.

directionalInsights.title: Users were unlikely to move existing conversations into a new app
directionalInsights.desc: Learners were unlikely to move established relationships into a dedicated language-learning messenger or convince friends and penpals to adopt another platform. A standalone app could still serve dedicated language-exchange discovery, but it was not the strongest first surface for conversations already happening elsewhere.

directionalInsights.title: Trust mattered as much as accuracy
directionalInsights.desc: Users know tools like Google Translate and DeepL can be "bad," but they think of bad translation as obvious broken grammar. They are less prepared for the more dangerous failure: a translation that looks fluent but changes tone, sounds unnatural, or subtly shifts intent.
directionalInsights.response: This shaped the Check experience. Reverse translation helps verify intent, while a glanceable naturalness score and teacher-style explanations help users understand whether a message sounds fluent in context — and why a translation works instead of simply trusting the output.

directionalInsights.title: Language nuance needed more controls
directionalInsights.desc: Studying language-specific translation needs revealed gaps around gender, homographs, script options, and contextual accuracy. Users needed more levers to shape meaning instead of settling for generic outputs.

execution.soWhatLead: Arcatext needed to close the gap between learning and
soWhat: It had to support relevant vocabulary and grammar, align with users’ interests, and enable practice through real or highly customizable conversations. That led to a keyboard-first strategy: a keyboard could support real conversations wherever they already happened, while also creating a foundation for future AI conversation tools, learning insights, and teacher-facing features.

productResponses.insight: Reduce friction in translating and understanding messages
productResponses.response: A consolidated keyboard experience that brought previously dispersed translation tasks into one writing surface.

The Check experience: reverse translation, a glanceable naturalness score concept, and teacher-style explanations to verify intent and avoid sending fluent-looking translations that subtly change meaning.
Part of the Reword feature — improving clarity, grammar, and naturalness even when the user is not translating into another language.

productResponses.insight: Lower the barrier for non-Roman script languages

productResponses.response: The "send a copy" concept, helping users preserve translated context across message exchanges.

productResponses.insight: Expose the learning value inside each message
productResponses.response: Synonyms, homograph clarification, sentence breakdown, and the Meaning Map concept.

productResponses.insight: Support contextual accuracy through user controls
productResponses.response: Gender controls, script options, clarification tools, and alternate phrasing.

competitive: After studying how translation tools are used during texting, it became clear that Arcatext could not only be a better translator — it had to be a better keyboard experience.

To earn a place in a daily-use interface, Arcatext needed to preserve familiar typing patterns, reduce friction, and outperform the basic translation workflows available through Apple Translate, Google Translate, DeepL, and Gboard. The product had to bridge experiential gaps while expanding beyond basic translation into confidence, context, and learning.

outcomes.title: Defined the product vision
outcomes.desc: Shaped Arcatext around a central insight: written conversation can become a personalized language-learning environment.

outcomes.title: Built a web-based proof of concept
outcomes.desc: Used HTML, JavaScript, and the ChatGPT API to validate the core Reword behavior before committing to the iOS keyboard.

outcomes.title: Designed the MVP translation keyboard
outcomes.desc: Shipped the first product surface inside existing messaging apps, supporting users in real communication contexts.

outcomes.title: Built the Reword experience
outcomes.desc: The core translation interaction — transforming an original message into a natural target-language version while preserving intent.

outcomes.title: Designed the Check experience
outcomes.desc: A pre-send verification flow that addresses a major trust issue: users copying translations without knowing whether the output matches intent.

outcomes.title: Added nuance controls
outcomes.desc: Speaker gender, recipient gender, group-chat gender, alphabet/script options, synonyms, reverse translation, and homograph clarification.

outcomes.title: Developed a scalable language-resource strategy
outcomes.desc: A structured translation pool for common messages and variations — a path toward faster responses, lower AI cost, and reusable linguistic data.

outcomes.title: Established the platform roadmap
outcomes.desc: The MVP keyboard is the foundation for AI conversation partners, progress insights, personalized learning paths, and teacher tools.

launch: Since Arcatext had not yet launched publicly, I treated measurement as part of launch readiness. I defined success around four questions.

launchQuestions.q: Can users translate with less friction?
Do they trust the output enough to send it?
Do clarification tools encourage learning behavior?
Can usage patterns support the subscription model?

caseStudies.titleLead: Focused looks at the most complex
intro: The Arcatext project page tells the high-level product story. Individual case studies go deeper on the specific design problems that shaped the product.

items.title: Translation Keyboard UX
items.question: How might we design a keyboard experience that feels lightweight, fast, and useful inside any messaging app?

items.title: Check View
items.question: How might we help users trust a translation before sending it?

items.title: Homograph Clarification
items.question: How might we help users control meaning when one word has multiple possible interpretations?

items.title: Gender-Aware Translation
items.question: How might we make gendered language visible and controllable without overwhelming the user?

items.title: Learning Insights
items.question: How might real conversations become measurable progress?

hero.title: Conversant
hero.subtitle: Enterprise desktop platform for call center and back-office servicing
hero.intro: Conversant was a mission-critical internal desktop platform for USAA employees who supported members through phone calls, chat, account servicing, and back-office workflows. The platform unified member context, communication tools, servicing alerts, product information, and internal applications into one workspace.

intro: Before Conversant, USAA employees relied on a fragmented set of tools to access member data and service accounts. A typical desktop could have 10–15 applications open at the same time, forcing representatives to move between disconnected systems while speaking with members or completing back-office work.

The main tool for accessing member data had not been meaningfully updated in roughly 20 years. It carried severe UX and technical performance issues, making the system difficult to learn, slow to navigate, and inconsistent with the needs of modern servicing work.

This created two very different user problems. New employees struggled to learn the system because the workflows were fragmented and hard to understand. Veteran employees could move faster, but often only because they had developed countless workarounds, memorized non-obvious pathways, and learned how to compensate for the system's limitations.

Conversant existed within that environment as a unified desktop workspace intended to help representatives stay oriented, access member context, and complete complex servicing tasks with more consistency and less fragmentation.
context.calloutLead: Repeated daily use under time pressure — not

intro: The product goal was to completely replace the previous internal member-data tool and consolidate as many fragmented secondary tools as possible into a simpler, more unified servicing environment.

Conversant needed to become the single launch point for employee workflows. Instead of asking representatives to know which separate system to open, where to find the right data, or how to move between disconnected tools, the platform needed to provide one consistent entry point for member servicing work.

At the same time, the system could not become rigid. Conversant was used for a wide variety of purposes depending on the employee's role and the reason for the member's call. A simple request like updating personal information might mean a quick visit to the information panel and an edit app, while opening a new account might mean using the product panel as a guided servicing or acquisition flow loaded in the center workspace.

This was the core idea behind Conversant supporting non-linear workflows. Representatives needed to move fluidly between member context, communication controls, documentation, product information, and task-specific tools without being forced through one rigid sequence.
designGoals: Replace the old member-data experience with a modern, unified workspace.
Consolidate fragmented tools and reduce unnecessary system-switching.
Create an intuitive layout and information architecture so employees could rely less on training, workarounds, and memorized paths.
Preserve the speed veteran employees had developed while introducing new efficiencies.
Support flexible, non-linear workflows across different employee roles and servicing scenarios.
Design for the 50th use, not just first-time comprehension — where long-term efficiency justifies the learning tradeoff.
Create reusable patterns that helped new Conversant experiences feel consistent with the larger platform.

intro: Conversant supported a wide range of USAA employee roles, including frontline phone representatives, banking reps, insurance reps, claims reps, specialists, back-office processors, managers, supervisors, chat employees, and employees who worked across multiple products or departments.

That range of roles created a major design challenge. Different employee groups had different workflows, priorities, and expectations for which information and tools needed to be immediately available. A banking representative, an insurance specialist, a claims employee, and a back-office processor might all need member context, but not always the same context, in the same order, or with the same level of urgency.

Because of that, Conversant needed to be highly configurable. The platform could not rely on one fixed layout or one universal set of tools. It needed to support role-specific experiences while still feeling like a coherent product. Part of that flexibility came through an admin tool that allowed representative managers to control aspects of the experience for their teams.

The previous servicing environment created friction at nearly every level of the employee experience. Tools were slow, workflows required too many clicks, and representatives often did not know where to find the information they needed. Data could be duplicated across systems, labels and navigation were inconsistent, and important member context could be difficult to read or missing from a given representative's view.

These issues created both usability and operational risks. New representatives struggled to onboard because the system did not clearly teach its own structure. Veteran representatives could often work faster, but only because they had built up informal institutional knowledge, memorized paths, and personal workarounds that were difficult to transfer to others.

problems.title: Slow, click-heavy tools
problems.desc: Workflows required too many clicks, and representatives often didn't know where to find the information they needed.

problems.title: Fragmented & duplicated data
problems.desc: Data was duplicated across systems, and labels and navigation were inconsistent from tool to tool.

problems.title: Hard to onboard
problems.desc: New representatives struggled because the system did not clearly teach its own structure.

problems.title: Tribal knowledge
problems.desc: Veterans worked fast only through memorized paths and personal workarounds that were difficult to transfer to others.

problems.title: Accessibility gaps
problems.desc: The old environment introduced accessibility problems across information architecture, color usage, and keyboard interaction — used repeatedly all day during live conversations.

closing: The product problem was not simply that employees needed "more information." They needed clearer, more complete, and more accessible information in a structure that supported accurate decisions, reduced unnecessary effort, and made complex servicing workflows easier to learn and execute.
problem.challengeLead: Replace a fragmented legacy toolset and support many nonlinear workflows — while protecting the speed
challenge: Conversant had to support employees doing cognitively demanding work under time pressure. Representatives needed to speak with members, understand account context, navigate multiple tools, manage phone or chat states, document outcomes, and complete servicing tasks without losing their place.
The design challenge was not simply to make the interface look cleaner. It was to make dense, operational workflows easier to understand and safer to execute across long work sessions.

intro: Conversant created a unified desktop application for member servicing. It brought together member personal information, member product line-up, servicing alerts, phone and chat features, and a center app browser for launching both native apps and web apps within the same workspace.

Instead of forcing representatives to treat each tool as a separate experience, Conversant gave them a shared frame for work: member context stayed accessible, applications opened inside a consistent container, and common controls behaved predictably across workflows.

highlights.title: Unified servicing desktop
highlights.desc: Member info, product line-up, servicing alerts, phone/chat, and a center app browser for native and web apps — all in one workspace.

highlights.title: Role-specific configuration
highlights.desc: Panel views could be toggled or rearranged by managers so teams worked from a focused, optimized view rather than one fixed layout.

highlights.title: A transition platform
highlights.desc: Reps kept access to tools through the integrated app browser while older internal apps were gradually migrated to run natively inside Conversant.

highlights.title: Efficiency over time
highlights.desc: Migration reduced load times, simplified workflows, and created a more consistent UX/UI across experiences that had previously felt fragmented.

highlights.title: Accessibility & keyboard support
highlights.desc: Improved accessibility and keyboard interaction for representatives using the app repeatedly throughout long work sessions.

intro: Conversant used a multi-panel desktop structure designed to keep member context, servicing tools, and communication controls available inside one workspace.

panels.title: Member tabs
panels.desc: The top of the app supported multiple open member profiles through tabs.

panels.title: Member information row
panels.desc: Below the tabs, a high-level context row let reps quickly confirm who they were helping and see essential profile details.

panels.title: Left member panel
panels.desc: Detailed member information, notifications, member details, and contact information.

panels.title: Left rail
panels.desc: Access to historical documents, previous activity, member settings, phone directory, and launch points for supporting tools.

panels.title: Right product panel
panels.desc: The member's product line-up, new product acquisition entry points, and help or knowledge content.

panels.title: Center workspace
panels.desc: The main launch point for native and web apps — edit experiences, servicing flows, acquisition workflows — and apps could pop out into separate windows.

panels.title: Communication tools
panels.desc: Phone and chat were collapsed by default and expandable when needed; the Phone Tool could move to a separate window when it blocked critical info.

panels.title: Notes
panels.desc: Notes could open inside the app or move to a separate window, supporting multitasking and documentation without leaving the environment.

panels.title: Configurable rails & panels
panels.desc: Side rails and panel views were configurable so managers could tailor the experience for different teams and roles.

closing: This structure created a recurring product design tension: every new feature needed to be useful in its own context, but it also had to behave coherently inside the larger platform. Much of my work focused on designing patterns that could scale beyond a single feature.

intro: The strongest stories to feature are the Product Panel redesign and Phone Tool interactions. These two areas show different dimensions of the work: one focused on information architecture, role-specific servicing needs, and product navigation; the other focused on high-pressure communication workflows, technical constraints, and interaction clarity during live member conversations.

features.title: Product Panel redesign
desc: The Product Panel helped representatives understand the member's relationship with USAA, review product line-up, and move into servicing or acquisition workflows. Because different employee roles needed different product information and entry points, the panel had to balance consistency with configurability.

This work shows how information architecture, role-specific needs, manager-controlled configuration, and product acquisition flows came together inside a dense enterprise workspace. It also shows how Conversant supported nonlinear workflows: sometimes the panel was only a reference point, while other times it became the launch point for a central servicing or acquisition app.

features.title: Phone Tool interactions
desc: The Phone Tool supported live member conversations inside Conversant. It included call controls, caller-specific actions, collapsed and expanded states, and the ability to move the tool into a separate window when the expanded state blocked information needed during a call.

This work shows how design decisions reduced ambiguity in a high-pressure workflow. It also shows how the team designed around an AWS-based backend rather than assuming every ideal interaction was technically possible.

intro: I worked across design research, UX/UI design, and product design. My role often extended beyond interface design into shaping scope and direction based on research findings.

I led generative research, usability testing, interviews, focus groups, research synthesis, product requirement creation, and shareouts with business and development partners. For each feature, I helped translate user needs and operational constraints into design direction, documentation, and implementation guidance.

Because Conversant served as a platform for many internal tools, I also coordinated with multiple teams designing web apps that lived inside the environment. I often served as an employee-user expert, helping teams adapt their designs for USAA employees rather than members and ensuring each app fit the workflows and usability needs of the internal servicing environment.
role.strategyTitle: Additional strategic work — digital fax tool
strategy: I also led the research and strategy for a digital fax tool integrated into the Conversant center app browser. This work included user interviews, research synthesis, product requirement recommendations, and journey maps that showed each step in the workflow for different user types and tasks.

The journey maps extended beyond the fax tool itself. They included the additional systems users needed to complete the broader workflow, which revealed that building a new standalone tool would not solve the real complexity employees faced. After mapping the full ecosystem, I recommended a different strategy: partner with the team that owned the main file-processing software and add our requirements as new features within their existing tool.

That strategy gave users a more streamlined workflow with less app switching and fewer repeated steps. It also helped USAA avoid the cost of standing up a new development team for a separate tool that would have added another application to an already complex ecosystem.

constraints.title: An existing, undocumented system
constraints.desc: The base app was already designed and developed. I had to work within an existing structure and an undocumented design system that was in use but not yet consistent.

constraints.title: Interconnected decisions
constraints.desc: A change that improved one workflow could affect another feature, role, or use case. Part of the work was identifying downstream impacts and judging acceptable compromises.

constraints.title: A large, diverse user base
constraints.desc: Many employee groups with nonlinear workflows and different priorities — sometimes needing a balanced default, other times customization so managers could tailor views.

constraints.title: Technical & timeline limits
constraints.desc: Timeline pressure and existing infrastructure shaped what could ship. Stronger solutions sometimes required backup plans and phased improvements.

constraints.title: AWS-based phone backend
constraints.desc: The Phone Tool had to work within the capabilities and limits of an AWS-based backend rather than a custom phone system designed from scratch.

constraints.title: Compliance & redaction
constraints.desc: Some user groups couldn't see complete member information, so the design had to support simplified or redacted views while preserving enough context to work accurately.

process: The pace of work on Conversant was fast and highly variable. Some features moved from intake to development handoff within a two-week sprint. Others took about a month from start to finish, including research, UX exploration, UI design, documentation, and handoff.

That velocity shaped how I approached the work. I had to quickly determine how much discovery was necessary, where research would reduce risk, when existing patterns could be reused, and when a feature needed deeper exploration before implementation.

The result was a modular design process. Not every project needed the same level of research or iteration, but every project still needed enough clarity for product, development, accessibility, and business partners to move forward with confidence.
measuring: When testing data was available, I evaluated design decisions through current-state vs. redesigned usability scores, representative confidence, task clarity, and qualitative feedback. The goal was to connect results to specific design decisions rather than simply claim that a redesign "tested better."

For a complex enterprise platform, success was not only visual polish. Success meant reducing confusion, improving confidence, supporting faster workflows, improving accessibility, and helping representatives complete complex servicing tasks with fewer unnecessary steps or workarounds.
collaboration: Conversant required collaboration across a large enterprise product ecosystem. I worked with the core Conversant design team, product teams, development teams, outside design teams building tools inside Conversant, MSR Labs for user testing coordination, accessibility partners, and design system partners.

Because many internal tools were being migrated or built into Conversant, collaboration often meant more than handing off screens. It meant helping other teams understand employee workflows, adapt member-facing patterns for internal users, and align their work with the larger Conversant platform experience.
designSystems: Conversant had its own design system for native desktop experiences, but that system was inherited, undocumented in places, and not always applied consistently when I joined. Part of the work involved identifying reusable patterns, clarifying component behavior, and helping the product become more consistent over time.

Web apps loaded inside the center browser used the standard USAA design system because it was more robust for web-based application patterns. This created a dual design-system environment: native Conversant patterns needed to feel consistent with the desktop shell, while embedded web apps needed to align with broader USAA standards.
The design challenge was knowing when to use existing patterns, when to adapt them, and when to create new platform-specific guidance for the needs of employee users.
demonstrates: Conversant demonstrates my ability to design inside complexity. The project required platform thinking, enterprise UX judgment, research-led product strategy, systems-level consistency, and practical collaboration across product, engineering, accessibility, and research partners.

It also shows that impactful design is not always a blank-slate redesign. In mature enterprise systems, the most valuable work often comes from improving specific workflows, strengthening patterns, reducing inconsistency, and helping teams move faster without making the user experience more fragmented.
impact.confidentiality: Because Conversant was an internal enterprise platform, sensitive details, member data, and internal workflows have been anonymized or recreated for public portfolio use.

hero.title: USAA Mobile
hero.subtitle: Native mobile redesign for USAA members
hero.intro: USAA Mobile was a redesign of USAA's native mobile app — the primary digital access point for members to view accounts, manage policies, complete servicing actions, and receive prioritized content. The work focused on simplifying a dated mobile experience, aligning app and web concepts, and creating a scalable structure for the member's financial relationship with USAA.
hero.roleIntro: I worked with my team across product strategy, UX structure, interaction design, and detailed feature design. We helped define the app's overall interaction model, the private and public homepage experiences, product cards, customization flows, and iPad patterns while navigating a large enterprise environment with multiple design, business, and development partners.

intro: The USAA app had not been meaningfully redesigned for years, even as member behavior was shifting toward mobile. Utilization of the app was rising quickly and overtaking the website, but the experience had not kept up with member expectations or business needs.

The old app was difficult to navigate, slow in terms of interaction and performance, and not structured around the information members needed most. It also underused USAA's understanding of individual member relationships. Product advertisements and advice were often irrelevant, which trained members to ignore content that could have been useful when presented with better timing and context.

At the same time, members still valued parts of the existing app. Quick access to servicing actions like transferring funds was useful, so the redesign could not simply discard everything. A critical part of the work was identifying what was already valuable and preserving that utility while rebuilding the surrounding structure.
context.calloutLead: Rising mobile usage on an app that hadn't kept up — modernize without

intro: The goal was to create a simpler, more modern app experience that helped members quickly understand and act on their relationship with USAA.
designGoals: Give members top-level access to the accounts, policies, and products they owned.
Make common servicing actions easier to find and complete.
Reduce navigation confusion and the avoidable call-center volume that came from it.
Align taxonomy, structure, and concepts across the app and website.
Use personalized content more effectively without creating banner blindness.
Support new product acquisition in a way that felt useful rather than intrusive.
Create a native interaction model that respected platform conventions while still expressing the USAA brand.
Build patterns that could scale across iOS, Android, and iPad.

intro: The redesign had to balance direct member needs with business goals. Members needed fast access to balances, policies, account summaries, servicing actions, claims, search, chat, and personalized reminders. They also needed control over how their accounts and policies appeared, especially when they had many products, hidden accounts, custom names, or grouped financial relationships.

The business needed the app to support acquisition, engagement, personalized content, and cross-platform consistency. That meant marketing and recommendations could not disappear from the experience, but they had to be integrated carefully. The product challenge was to present business content in a way that respected the member's task instead of competing with it.

The old app was trying to do too much without a clear structure. Important member-owned products were not always easy to access at the top level, while less relevant content could distract from the reason a member opened the app.

problems.title: Hard to find the basics
problems.desc: Members had trouble finding basic account and policy information.

problems.title: Avoidable call volume
problems.desc: Navigation patterns increased reliance on call centers for avoidable servicing needs.

problems.title: Underused personalization
problems.desc: Personalized data was underused, so content often felt generic or irrelevant.

problems.title: No single view of everything
problems.desc: The structure did not fully support members who wanted to see everything they owned in one place.

problems.title: Lagging native acquisition
problems.desc: Native product acquisition was not keeping up with the broader shift toward mobile usage.

closing: The design problem was not only visual modernization. The deeper problem was helping members move through a complex financial relationship quickly, confidently, and with fewer unnecessary steps.

intro: The redesigned app centered the experience around a new Home structure. Home became the primary place where members could see their products, take common actions, access personalized reminders, and discover relevant offers or guidance.

We organized Home around product category cards for Banking, Insurance, Investing, and Advice & Services. Each card could contain related accounts, policies, actions, and product suggestions. This gave members a clearer model of their relationship with USAA while still giving the business a structured place for acquisition and engagement.

Home also included activity cards: personalized content generated from a member's relationship with USAA or recent activity. Examples included upcoming bill reminders and travel notices. Instead of treating all content as equal, the experience prioritized what was most likely to matter to the member.

The broader app structure used a simple tab model: Home, Search, and Chat. Home was the main destination for owned products, servicing entry points, personalized content, and acquisition. Search and Chat supported members who needed to find something directly or get help.

highlights.title: Product cards
highlights.desc: Banking, Insurance, Investing, and Advice & Services — each grouping related accounts, policies, actions, and suggestions.

highlights.title: Activity cards
highlights.desc: Timely, personalized content like upcoming bills and travel notices, prioritized by what's most likely to matter now.

highlights.title: Home · Search · Chat
highlights.desc: A simple three-tab model: Home for products and content, Search for direct paths, Chat for help-oriented moments.

structure: The redesigned app used Home as the center of gravity. From Home, members could branch into lower-level account summaries, policy pages, the claims center, servicing flows, modals, and personalized content experiences.

Product cards provided a flexible structure for owned products, related actions, and product suggestions. Activity cards provided a separate pattern for timely, personalized content. Together, these patterns helped separate "my stuff," "things I can do," and "things USAA thinks may matter now."

This separation was important because the old app blurred too many content types together. By giving each type of content a more specific role, the experience could be more predictable for members and more scalable for future business needs.

contentRoles.title: My stuff
contentRoles.desc: Accounts, policies, and products the member owns.

contentRoles.title: Things I can do
contentRoles.desc: Servicing actions like transfers and deposits, near the products they relate to.

contentRoles.title: What may matter now
contentRoles.desc: Prioritized, personalized reminders and relevant suggestions.

intro: The biggest product tension was equal access versus prioritization.

Members had a wide variety of relationships with USAA. Some had only a few accounts or policies, while others had dozens or even close to one hundred products across banking, insurance, investments, and aggregated accounts. Members could also hide accounts, create groups, customize names, and see different totals depending on their product mix.

approaches.title: Selection + deeper page
approaches.desc: Show only a selection of accounts on the first level, with everything else on a deeper page.

approaches.title: Expandable cards
approaches.desc: Let cards expand so members could view more without leaving Home.

approaches.title: Everything exposed
approaches.desc: The more controversial direction: expose everything by default on the first level of Home.

closing: The final direction leaned toward showing member-owned products at the top level. Member feedback indicated that members wanted to see all of their products in one place. Business concerns were balanced by the reality that most members had relatively few accounts, so product cards would not usually push activity cards, marketing, or other content too far out of view. Members with very large product relationships already had deep engagement with USAA, making the tradeoff more acceptable.

This decision created a clearer, more member-centered structure. It also simplified the integration of customization features because the app could treat Home as the main place where members organized and accessed their stuff.

intro: Four areas where I worked through the structure and details of the redesign — individually and collaboratively.

features.title: Organization and interaction
desc: We incorporated concepts and structures from the redesigned web homepage to create better alignment across platforms. This helped members build a more consistent mental model whether they were using the website or native app.

The app's top-level structure became Home, Search, and Chat. Home carried the main product relationship and personalized content. Search gave members a direct path when they knew what they wanted. Chat created a persistent support path for help-oriented moments.
This structure reduced the need for deep navigation and helped the app behave more like a task-oriented native experience.

features.title: Home and card model
desc: Home organized the member experience into product cards and activity cards.

Product cards grouped Banking, Insurance, Investing, and Advice & Services. These cards gave members a place to view accounts and policies, take related actions, and discover relevant product suggestions. Actions such as transferring funds or depositing money could live near the products they related to instead of being buried elsewhere.

Activity cards handled personalized reminders and relationship-based content, such as upcoming bills or travel notices. This helped create a more useful form of personalization because content appeared as part of the member's current context instead of as disconnected advertising.

features.title: Customization
desc: A major part of the work was integrating existing customization capabilities into the redesigned app. The customization experience needed to feel modern on native platforms while supporting a complex set of behaviors.

This work required interaction analysis because customization could easily conflict with the new Home structure. The goal was to give members control without making the main product experience harder to understand.
bullets: Choosing where the customization entry point belonged on Home.
Determining the right view type for customization.
Rationalizing multiple isolated tools versus one unified experience.
Reordering accounts, policies, groups, and entire product categories.
Creating, deleting, and adding products to groups.
Migrating "view all" and "show hidden" into a cleaner interaction model.

features.title: iPad
desc: After the core mobile app structure was defined, iPad introduced a new design challenge. The app needed a system of structures, view types, and interactions that made better use of the larger platform while still connecting back to the broader USAA Mobile model.
This work helped extend the app from a phone-first experience into a flexible native ecosystem.
bullets: Persistent full-screen controls across the navigation stack.
Column-width rules for a strong experience without complex logic.

intro: My team and I drastically simplified the UX and helped make the app and website feel like familiar experiences. We aligned taxonomy, structure, and concepts across platforms, defined the app's overall interaction model, and worked through the heavily trafficked public and private homepage experiences.
work: Helping define the app's organization and interaction model.
Designing Home concepts, product card structures, activity-card logic, and destination patterns.
Exploring how member products should be exposed, grouped, expanded, hidden, and customized.
Integrating existing customization features into a modern native app experience.
Working through platform-specific behavior for iOS, Android, and iPad.
Navigating communication and collaboration across multiple design teams, business partners, development partners, and executive stakeholders.
role.closing: This work also influenced how USAA could embrace native platform conventions while developing a new native design language.

process: The work required alignment across a large product organization. Multiple design teams were involved, and the project included conflicting viewpoints from business and development partners as well as direct executive involvement.

The process included understanding member feedback and data, identifying what was working in the old app, defining member needs and business goals, exploring competing structures, reviewing technical constraints, and iterating toward a model that could scale.

This was also a communication challenge. Because the redesign touched major parts of the app and overlapped with web strategy, design language, business goals, and technical implementation, success depended on making tradeoffs visible and helping stakeholders understand why the chosen model served both members and the business.
measuring: The redesign aimed to improve success through clarity, speed, and usefulness. Stronger outcomes would show up through easier access to accounts and policies, reduced navigation confusion, better engagement with relevant personalized content, and fewer avoidable servicing moments that pushed members to call centers.

For this kind of product, success was not only whether the interface looked more modern. Success meant that members could open the app, understand their relationship with USAA, act on the thing they came to do, and discover useful next steps without feeling distracted or lost.
demonstrates: USAA Mobile demonstrates my ability to simplify a large consumer-facing product without ignoring business complexity. The work required platform thinking, native mobile interaction design, cross-platform taxonomy alignment, personalization strategy, product-card information architecture, and collaboration across a complex enterprise environment.

It also shows how product design often depends on disciplined tradeoffs. The strongest solution was not simply the cleanest screen or the most minimal first level. It was the structure that best matched member expectations, supported business needs, preserved valuable existing behaviors, and created a scalable foundation for future app experiences.
impact.confidentiality: Because this work involved USAA's internal product strategy, member data, and unreleased or proprietary design decisions, sensitive details and visuals have been anonymized or recreated for public portfolio use.
